text: The video shows a completed cholecystectomy with a visible Morgagni hernia located on either side of the falciform ligament.
The surgeon begins the division of the falciform ligament using monopolar diathermy, carefully cutting through the ligament tissue.
The surgeon dissects the hernia defect, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues to prepare for repair.
The hernial content is visible, with the transverse colon positioned within the hernia sac.
The surgeon meticulously dissects and removes excess fat that has migrated into the Morgagni's hernia, focusing on both superior and inferior aspects of the hernia site.
The surgeon dissects the margins of the hernia sac, revealing the defect and hernia. The fat within the hernia sac appears to have a distinct color compared to surrounding tissues.
The surgeon removes the last portion of the hernia sac and divides the falciform ligament to free the inferior surface of the diaphragm, preparing the area for repair with barbed sutures.

The surgeon pulls the suture to obliterate the hernia defect, having prepared the raw surface. A continuous suture is applied from the right side to the left side, visibly repairing the defect. The repair is completed with a two-layer suture technique, without the need for mesh, as the hernia is non-dependent.
The surgeon progresses from left to right, passing multiple sutures to reinforce the hernia defect with a second layer of suturing. The sutures are carefully pulled out to secure the repair.

The surgeon takes the last suture bite in a reverse manner to self-lock it, eliminating the need for formal knotting. The suture is then pulled and divided with scissors. The completed cholecystectomy site is visible, indicating the end of the surgery.
